goal_title_suggestions: Learn to Day Trade Without Losing Real Money
Build a Paper Trading System in 90 Days
Master One Setup Before Ever Going Live

variants: Go to thinkorswim.com right now, click 'Open Account,' and choose the paperMoney option — do not enter any real payment info.
Navigate to thinkorswim.com, select the paperMoney paper trading account, and complete the signup form — no real money required.
Pull up thinkorswim.com on your browser and start the paperMoney signup flow — finish the form before closing this tab.
tasks.why: You need a live paper trading environment today — everything else builds on this account.

variants: Search 'SPY stock chart' on TradingView, open the 5-minute candlestick chart, and screenshot it so you have a reference for what a normal session looks like.
Open TradingView, type SPY in the search bar, switch the chart to 5-minute candles, and save a screenshot to your phone or desktop.
Go to TradingView, load SPY on a 5-minute timeframe, and take a screenshot of the current chart — label it 'Day 1 baseline' in your files.
tasks.why: SPY is your daily reference ticker — getting eyes on it today starts building visual familiarity.

variants: Watch exactly ONE YouTube video on reading candlestick charts (search 'candlestick basics for beginners' and pick one under 8 minutes), then write down 3 specific things you learned in a notes app.
Find a candlestick chart explainer video under 8 minutes on YouTube, watch it fully, and record 3 takeaways in your notes — not vague, specific: name the candle types you'll use.
Search YouTube for 'candlestick chart beginner' and watch one video under 8 minutes, then open a notes doc and type 3 concrete facts you now know that you didn't before.
tasks.why: Candlesticks are the language of every chart — you need the vocabulary before anything else.

variants: Log into your thinkorswim paperMoney account and confirm your starting paper balance — write that number down somewhere you'll keep it.
Open thinkorswim paperMoney, find your account balance on the main screen, and note it in your trading journal or a notes doc.
Launch the thinkorswim paperMoney platform and locate your starting balance — record it with today's date so you have a baseline.
tasks.why: Knowing your starting number is step one of tracking real performance over 90 days.

variants: Open Investopedia, search 'support and resistance,' read the first article for 4 minutes, then close it and write a one-sentence definition in your own words.
Go to Investopedia, look up support and resistance levels, spend 4 minutes on the article, then write your own one-sentence definition without looking at the page.
Search Investopedia for 'support resistance trading,' read for 4 minutes, then copy nothing — write your own definition in your notes from memory.
tasks.why: Support and resistance are the two most used concepts in day trading — define them before you see them on a chart.

variants: Pull up SPY on the thinkorswim paperMoney chart, zoom to the last 5 trading days on a daily timeframe, and draw a horizontal line at what looks like the biggest support level — label it 'Day 2 support.'
Open SPY on your thinkorswim paperMoney chart using the daily timeframe, look at the last 5 sessions, and draw one horizontal support line at the most obvious bounce point — save the chart.
In thinkorswim paperMoney, load SPY on a daily chart covering the past 5 days, identify the price level where SPY bounced hardest, draw a line there, and label it with today's date.
tasks.why: Drawing lines on a real chart — not just reading about them — is how the concept locks in.

variants: Create a Google Sheet titled 'Trading Journal' with columns: Date, Ticker, Entry Price, Exit Price, Shares, P/L, Setup, Notes — do nothing else in it yet.
Open Google Sheets, name a new file 'Trading Journal,' and add these column headers in row 1: Date, Ticker, Entry, Exit, Shares, P/L, Setup, Notes.
Set up your trading journal in Google Sheets right now — new file, name it 'Trading Journal,' and put these 8 headers in row 1: Date, Ticker, Entry, Exit, Shares, P/L, Setup, Notes.
tasks.why: Your journal is the most important tool in this path — setting it up today means it's ready when you start paper trading.

variants: Look up 'market orders vs limit orders' on Investopedia, read for 3 minutes, then go into thinkorswim paperMoney and find where you would place a limit order on SPY — just locate the field, don't submit anything.
Search Investopedia for the difference between market and limit orders, read for 3 minutes, then open thinkorswim paperMoney and navigate to the order entry screen for SPY without placing a trade.
Spend 3 minutes on Investopedia reading about market and limit orders, then log into your paperMoney account and find the order ticket for SPY — identify where limit price is entered.
tasks.why: Knowing order types before you place your first trade prevents costly beginner mistakes.

variants: Search YouTube for 'regular trading hours vs pre-market explained' and watch one video under 6 minutes, then write in your journal: what time does the regular session open and close, and what happens in pre-market.
Find a YouTube explainer under 6 minutes on stock market hours, watch it, then write three facts in your notes: market open time, market close time, and one risk of trading pre-market.
Watch one YouTube video under 6 minutes about stock market trading hours, then record in your trading journal: open time, close time, and why you'll focus only on regular hours as a beginner.
tasks.why: Day traders live and die by session timing — knowing the schedule is non-negotiable before your first paper trade.

variants: Open TradingView, load QQQ on a 1-minute chart, and identify three green candles and three red candles — name what each means (buyers won vs. sellers won) in your notes.
Pull up QQQ on TradingView using a 1-minute timeframe and point out three bullish and three bearish candles in the chart — write a one-word label for each in your notes doc.
Go to TradingView, load a 1-minute QQQ chart, and find 3 green candles and 3 red candles — write in your notes: 'green = buyers closed above open, red = sellers closed below open.'
tasks.why: Reading candle color and direction instantly is the first skill that separates traders from watchers.

variants: Open AAPL on thinkorswim paperMoney using a 5-minute chart, draw a horizontal resistance line at the most obvious recent high, and draw a support line at the most obvious recent low — screenshot both.
In your thinkorswim paperMoney account, load AAPL on a 5-minute chart, identify the clearest recent high and low, and draw horizontal lines at both levels — save a screenshot.

Launch thinkorswim paperMoney, pull up AAPL on a 5-minute timeframe, and draw one resistance line at the recent peak and one support line at the recent low — take a screenshot and label it 'Day 4 AAPL levels.'
tasks.why: Practicing support and resistance drawing on a real chart turns theory into muscle memory.

variants: Watch one YouTube video under 8 minutes titled something like 'VWAP explained for day traders,' write 3 takeaways in your notes, then find the VWAP indicator on TradingView and add it to a SPY 5-minute chart.

Search YouTube for 'VWAP day trading basics,' watch a video under 8 minutes, take 3 notes, then open TradingView and add the VWAP indicator to a SPY 5-minute chart so you can see what it looks like live.
Find and watch a YouTube video under 8 minutes explaining VWAP, write 3 specific facts in your journal, then go to TradingView and overlay VWAP on a SPY 5-minute chart — screenshot the result.
tasks.why: VWAP is the single most referenced intraday indicator — seeing it on a real chart today plants the visual seed.

variants: Open your Trading Journal Google Sheet and add a row at the bottom: today's date, 'SPY,' and in the Notes column write the current support and resistance levels you'd use if you were trading today.

Go to your Trading Journal sheet and fill in a row with today's date and SPY — enter the price levels you'd mark as support and resistance based on yesterday's chart work, even though you're not trading yet.
Pull up your Trading Journal and log a practice observation row: date, ticker SPY, and two price levels — one support, one resistance — based on what you see on the chart right now.
tasks.why: Using the journal before you trade builds the habit so it's automatic when real paper trades begin.

variants: Search Investopedia for 'opening range breakout strategy,' read the article for 4 minutes, then write a 2-sentence definition in your notes: what it is and when it triggers.
Look up opening range breakout on Investopedia, read for 4 minutes, then close the tab and write in your notes — in 2 sentences — what the setup is and what specific price action triggers it.

Read the Investopedia page on opening range breakout for 4 minutes, then document in your trading journal: what the 'opening range' is (first 15 or 30 minutes) and what a breakout above it looks like.
tasks.why: Opening range breakout is one of the three setups you'll choose from — understanding it now starts the decision process.

variants: Open SPY on thinkorswim paperMoney on a 5-minute chart and mark the first 30 minutes of today's or yesterday's session with a bracket — identify the high and low of that range and write both prices in your journal.
In thinkorswim paperMoney, load a 5-minute SPY chart, find the first 30-minute candles of the most recent session, and highlight the range high and low — log both numbers in your Trading Journal.

Pull up a SPY 5-minute chart in thinkorswim paperMoney, isolate the first 30 minutes of the last trading session, record the range high and low in your journal, and draw horizontal lines at both levels.
tasks.why: Identifying the opening range visually on a real chart is the most important precursor to trading the breakout setup.

variants: Open your Trading Journal and verify it has columns for Setup — if it doesn't, add a Setup column now so you're ready to log paper trades.
Check your Trading Journal Google Sheet for a 'Setup' column and add one if it's missing — you'll need it starting day 15.
Pull up your Trading Journal and confirm the column headers match: Date, Ticker, Entry, Exit, Shares, P/L, Setup, Notes — add any missing ones now.
tasks.why: The journal needs to be complete before paper trades begin — small fix now, no friction later.

variants: Watch one YouTube video under 6 minutes about the 9 EMA and 20 EMA crossover strategy, write 2 takeaways in your notes, and add both EMAs to a QQQ 5-minute TradingView chart.
Search YouTube for '9 EMA 20 EMA crossover day trading' and watch a video under 6 minutes — then open TradingView, load QQQ on 5 minutes, and overlay both EMA lines on the chart.

Find a YouTube video under 6 minutes explaining the 9/20 EMA crossover, take 2 notes, then go to TradingView and add EMA 9 and EMA 20 to a QQQ 5-minute chart — screenshot the chart with both lines showing.
tasks.why: The 9/20 EMA crossover is your second setup option — seeing both EMAs on a live chart makes the choice concrete.

variants: Open thinkorswim paperMoney, load QQQ on a 5-minute chart, add the 9 EMA and 20 EMA, and find a point in the last 5 sessions where the 9 crossed above the 20 — circle it and write the date/time in your journal.

In thinkorswim paperMoney, pull up QQQ on 5 minutes, add both EMA 9 and EMA 20, scroll back through the last 5 sessions, and identify one bullish crossover — log the date, time, and what happened to price after.

Load QQQ on a 5-minute thinkorswim paperMoney chart with EMA 9 and EMA 20 overlaid, scan the past 5 trading days for a crossover where the 9 moved above the 20, and record the date and price action outcome in your journal.
tasks.why: Finding a crossover in real historical data teaches you what a valid signal actually looks like.

variants: Open your Trading Journal, review every entry you've made this week, and write one sentence at the bottom: what's the single most useful thing you learned in days 1-6.
Go through your Trading Journal from Day 1 to today, re-read every note, and write a one-sentence weekly summary — the most actionable concept you picked up.
Review your full Trading Journal — every note from days 1 through 6 — then add a 'Week 1 Review' row and write one sentence: what stuck, what's still fuzzy.
tasks.why: Weekly review is a scheduled practice — it compounds faster than daily new input.

variants: Compare the three setups you've studied — opening range breakout, VWAP bounce, and 9/20 EMA crossover — and pick ONE that you'll use for all your paper trades starting day 15, writing your choice and a one-sentence reason in your journal.

Write in your Trading Journal which of the three setups you're committing to: opening range breakout, VWAP bounce, or 9/20 EMA crossover — one choice only, with one sentence explaining why you picked it.
Make the setup decision today: opening range breakout, VWAP bounce, or 9/20 EMA crossover — write your pick in the journal along with one sentence on why it appeals to you more than the others.
tasks.why: Choosing one setup and sticking to it is what separates disciplined traders from strategy-hoppers.

variants: Open SPY on thinkorswim paperMoney on a 5-minute chart, add whichever indicator matches your chosen setup (VWAP, EMA 9/20, or nothing for ORB), and spend 7 minutes studying the last 3 trading sessions with that lens — write 2 observations in your journal.

Load SPY on a 5-minute chart in thinkorswim paperMoney, apply the indicator for your chosen setup, and scan 3 previous sessions looking for spots where your setup would have triggered — log 2 specific examples.

In thinkorswim paperMoney, pull up SPY on 5 minutes, configure your chart for your chosen setup (add VWAP, or EMA 9+20, or mark the opening range), then study 3 recent sessions and write 2 examples of where the setup appeared.
tasks.why: Seeing your chosen setup on real historical data is the bridge between theory and your first paper trade.

variants: Write today's setup name at the top of a fresh notes page — just the name and three bullet points of what must be true before you enter a trade using it.
Open your notes and write your chosen setup name plus 3 entry criteria in bullet form — the exact conditions that must be met before you press buy in paperMoney.
In your Trading Journal, add a tab called 'Rules' and write your chosen setup name plus 3 conditions that must be present for a valid trade signal.
tasks.why: Writing your rules before you trade makes them real — undocumented rules get broken.

variants: Search Investopedia for 'stop loss order,' read for 3 minutes, then write in your journal: where would you put a stop loss on your chosen setup (specific: below support, below VWAP, below EMA, etc.).
Read the Investopedia article on stop loss orders for 3 minutes, then add a row to your Rules tab: 'Stop Loss = [specific level]' based on your chosen setup.
Spend 3 minutes on Investopedia reading about stop loss orders, then document in your journal exactly where your stop will go when trading your chosen setup — be specific, not vague.
tasks.why: Defining your stop loss before your first trade removes the emotional decision in the moment.

variants: Open AAPL on thinkorswim paperMoney on a 5-minute chart and scan the last 5 sessions for 3 examples where your chosen setup would have triggered — write each in your journal: date, time, entry price, and whether the trade worked.
In thinkorswim paperMoney, load a 5-minute AAPL chart, find 3 historical moments where your setup signal appeared, and log each in your journal with entry price, stop level, and trade outcome.

Pull up AAPL on a 5-minute thinkorswim paperMoney chart, go back through the last week of data, and identify 3 spots where your setup would have fired — record each with a date, price, and 'win/loss if traded' assessment.
tasks.why: Back-reading charts for your setup builds pattern recognition without any real or paper money at risk.

variants: Open your Rules tab in the Trading Journal and add a 'Target' row — write the specific price level or percentage gain where you'll take profit on your setup.

Add a profit target rule to your Trading Journal: after your stop loss entry, write exactly where you'll exit a winning paper trade — be specific (e.g., 'next resistance level' or '2x the stop distance').
Go to your Rules section and define your take-profit level for your chosen setup — write it down with a specific rule, not 'when it feels right.'
tasks.why: A take-profit rule completes your trade plan — entry, stop, and target, all defined before you trade.

variants: Open QQQ on thinkorswim paperMoney on a 5-minute chart, apply your setup's indicator(s), and scan 5 sessions for setups that would have hit your stop — log 2 losing examples in your journal with the price and what happened after.
In thinkorswim paperMoney, load QQQ on 5 minutes with your setup configured, find 2 historical examples where the setup triggered but price hit your stop, and write both in your journal.

Pull up a QQQ 5-minute chart in thinkorswim paperMoney, look through the last 5 sessions, and find 2 instances where your setup appeared but failed — record date, entry price, stop price, and outcome in your journal.
tasks.why: Studying losses before you've lost paper money trains discipline before it hurts.

variants: Place your first paper trade in thinkorswim paperMoney: buy 10 shares of SPY using a limit order at the current price, then immediately set a stop loss 1% below your entry — screenshot the open position.

Execute your first practice paper trade in thinkorswim paperMoney right now: open a 10-share SPY position with a limit order, set a stop 1% below entry, and screenshot the positions tab showing it open.

In thinkorswim paperMoney, place a paper trade — 10 shares of SPY, limit order at ask price, stop loss 1% below — and take a screenshot of your open position. This is a practice trade, not tied to your setup yet.
tasks.why: The mechanics of placing and managing a paper order need to be muscle memory before day 15.

variants: Log yesterday's practice paper trade in your Trading Journal: entry price, today's exit price (or current price if still open), and P/L in dollars — calculate it manually, don't just look at the platform.
Go to your Trading Journal and fill in a row for the Day 9 paper trade: date, SPY, entry price, current or exit price, shares (10), and dollar P/L — do the math yourself.

Open your Trading Journal and complete the entry for your first paper trade from yesterday: entry, exit (or close at current if still open), P/L calculated by hand, and a one-word note on how the trade felt to watch.
tasks.why: Logging every trade by hand, not just trusting the platform summary, builds accounting discipline.

variants: Open SPY on thinkorswim paperMoney on a 1-minute chart and watch it live for 5 minutes during market hours — count how many candles close green vs. red and write the tally in your notes.
During regular market hours, pull up a 1-minute SPY chart in thinkorswim paperMoney and watch it for 5 minutes without trading — tally green vs. red closes and note any visible patterns.
Set a 5-minute timer, open SPY on a 1-minute thinkorswim paperMoney chart during market hours, and watch price move in real time — write 2 observations about what you noticed in your journal.
tasks.why: Watching a live chart without trading forces you to observe rather than react — a skill beginners skip.

variants: Place a second practice paper trade in thinkorswim paperMoney — this time on QQQ, 10 shares, limit order, with a stop loss and a written target level in your journal before you submit the order.
Execute a second paper trade in thinkorswim paperMoney: 10 shares of QQQ, limit order at the current price, stop and target both written in your journal BEFORE you place the order.
Open thinkorswim paperMoney, write your entry, stop, and target for a QQQ 10-share paper trade in your journal, then place the order — stop must be set the moment the order fills.
tasks.why: Requiring yourself to write the plan before placing the order is the habit that prevents emotional trading.

variants: Open your Trading Journal and close out any open paper trades logged on days 9 or 10 — exit them at current market price in thinkorswim paperMoney and log the final P/L in the journal.
Check thinkorswim paperMoney for any open paper positions from days 9-10, close them at market price, and update your Trading Journal with the final exit price and P/L.
Log into thinkorswim paperMoney, close every open paper trade from earlier this week at market price, and complete the journal rows with final exit prices and dollar results.
tasks.why: Leaving trades open indefinitely is a bad habit — closing and logging completes the feedback loop.

variants: Search Investopedia for 'risk reward ratio trading,' read for 3 minutes, then calculate the risk/reward on your two practice trades from this week — write each ratio in your journal.
Look up risk-to-reward ratio on Investopedia, read for 3 minutes, then go back to your journal entries for days 9 and 10 and calculate the R:R for each trade.

Spend 3 minutes reading about risk-reward ratios on Investopedia, then open your journal and calculate: for each of your two practice trades, what was the risk (entry to stop) vs. reward (entry to target)?
tasks.why: Every setup needs a positive expected R:R — calculating it on your first trades builds that discipline early.

variants: Open AAPL on thinkorswim paperMoney on a 5-minute chart and scan the last 2 days for 3 moments where your chosen setup appeared — log each in your journal with a verdict: would you have taken it based on your rules?
In thinkorswim paperMoney, load AAPL on a 5-minute chart covering the past 2 sessions, find 3 instances of your setup, and write in your journal whether each one met your entry criteria and why.

Pull up AAPL on a 5-minute thinkorswim paperMoney chart, look at the last 2 trading days, and identify 3 setup signals — log each with a yes/no: does it meet every rule you've written, and explain one reason.
tasks.why: Filtering setups against your rules before trading them is exactly what separates disciplined from impulsive.

variants: Open TradingView, add SPY on a 15-minute chart, and spend 3 minutes identifying the current trend direction — write in your notes: uptrend, downtrend, or sideways, with one visual reason.
Pull up SPY on TradingView using a 15-minute timeframe and write a trend direction verdict in your notes — up, down, or sideways — with one specific candle or price action reason.
Load a 15-minute SPY chart on TradingView, study it for 3 minutes, then write your trend assessment in your journal: what direction is price moving and what's your visual evidence?
tasks.why: Trading with trend beats trading against it — daily trend identification becomes a pre-trade checklist item.

variants: Look up 'volume in day trading' on Investopedia, read for 4 minutes, then go to your thinkorswim paperMoney chart and add volume bars to a SPY 5-minute chart — note whether today's volume is above or below the average bar.

Search Investopedia for how volume confirms trading signals, read for 4 minutes, then add volume to a SPY 5-minute thinkorswim paperMoney chart and write one observation about volume levels in your notes.

Read the Investopedia article on trading volume for 4 minutes, then open thinkorswim paperMoney, add volume bars to SPY on a 5-minute chart, and write in your journal: is volume confirming or contradicting today's price move?
tasks.why: High-volume setups are more reliable — you need to see volume on the chart before paper trades begin.

variants: Add a 'Volume' column to your Trading Journal Rules tab and write a rule for it: what minimum volume condition must be met before you take a trade (e.g., 'volume must be above average on breakout candle').

Update your Trading Journal Rules section with a volume filter — write a specific rule about volume (e.g., 'entry candle volume must be at least 1.5x the 20-bar average') that you'll apply to every paper trade.
Open your Rules section and add a volume requirement: define the exact volume condition your setup must show before you're allowed to enter a paper trade — write it in a single clear sentence.
tasks.why: A volume rule added before you start paper trading means it's enforced from trade 1, not retrofitted after losses.

variants: Open your Trading Journal Rules tab and count the total number of rules you've written — if you have fewer than 5 specific entry/exit rules, add what's missing right now.
Review your Rules section in the Trading Journal and make sure you have at least these 5 items documented: setup name, entry trigger, stop level, profit target, and volume condition.
Pull up your Rules tab and do a quick audit: setup name, entry condition, stop, target, and volume filter — if any are missing or vague, rewrite them to be specific before tomorrow.
tasks.why: Day 15 is your first real paper trade — your rules need to be complete and unambiguous before that session.

variants: Watch exactly one YouTube video under 7 minutes about your specific chosen setup (search '[your setup name] day trading example'), write 2 notes in your journal, and check whether the trader's rules match yours.
Find a YouTube video under 7 minutes showing a real trade using your chosen setup, watch it, and compare the trader's entry/exit rules to what you've written — note any difference in your journal.
Search YouTube for a live trade example of your setup (under 7 minutes), watch it fully, then write in your journal: 2 things the trader did that match your rules and 1 thing they did differently.
tasks.why: Seeing your setup executed by someone else validates your understanding before you do it yourself.

variants: Open SPY on thinkorswim paperMoney on a 5-minute chart, go through the last 3 trading sessions, and run your entire checklist on every potential signal — log each in your journal with a pass/fail for each rule.
In thinkorswim paperMoney, load SPY on 5 minutes and scan the last 3 sessions applying every rule from your Rules tab to each potential signal — write pass/fail for each condition in your journal.

Pull up SPY 5-minute chart in thinkorswim paperMoney, look through 3 recent sessions, and for each setup signal that appears, check it against every single rule you've written — log results in your journal.
tasks.why: Running your full checklist on historical signals is the final dry run before live paper trading starts.

variants: Review your Trading Journal from days 1-13, then write a 'Week 2 Review' entry: what rules are clear, what's still uncertain, and one thing you want to improve in week 3.
Go through every Trading Journal entry from the past two weeks and write a Week 2 Summary row: what concepts are solid, what needs more chart time, and your biggest open question.
Read back through your full journal from days 1-13, then add a review entry: rate your rule clarity out of 5, name the most valuable concept you learned, and write one weak spot to address.
tasks.why: Bi-weekly review days are how progress compounds — this is a scheduled practice, not optional.

variants: Open thinkorswim paperMoney, load your chosen ticker (SPY or QQQ) on a 5-minute chart with your setup configured, and simulate tomorrow's trade plan: write the exact entry price you'd look for, your stop, and your target if the setup triggers at market open.

In thinkorswim paperMoney, configure your chart for your setup, then write a trade plan for tomorrow in your journal: entry trigger, entry price zone, stop loss level, and take-profit level — everything before the session starts.

Set up your thinkorswim paperMoney chart for your chosen setup and write tomorrow's pre-market plan in your journal: what price level triggers entry, where the stop goes, and where you'll exit with a gain.
tasks.why: Writing a trade plan the night before is the professional habit that prevents reactive decisions at open.

variants: Do a final chart review: open SPY and QQQ on 5-minute thinkorswim paperMoney charts, apply your setup indicators, and note in your journal whether each ticker shows a potential setup for tomorrow's session.

Run a pre-session scan: load SPY and QQQ in thinkorswim paperMoney on 5-minute charts with your setup configured, and write a one-line assessment for each — 'setup possible' or 'no clean setup tomorrow.'

Open both SPY and QQQ in thinkorswim paperMoney using 5-minute charts, configure your indicators, and write your pre-market assessment in the journal: does either ticker show conditions favorable for your setup tomorrow?
tasks.why: Scanning the night before gives you a context-informed starting point rather than scrambling at open.

variants: Open your Trading Journal and re-read your full rules list before touching any chart — confirm every rule is there and you can state each one from memory before the session starts.
Before opening any chart today, go to your Trading Journal Rules tab and read every rule aloud or silently — if you can't recall what any rule means, clarify it now.
Review your complete ruleset in the Trading Journal before logging into thinkorswim — rules, stop placement, target, volume filter — all must be clear in your head before you see a single candle.
tasks.why: Reading your rules before every session is the ritual that keeps discipline from slipping.

variants: Open thinkorswim paperMoney, set up your chart for your chosen ticker and timeframe, and wait for your setup to appear — if it doesn't appear in 5 minutes of watching, write 'No setup today' in your journal and do not trade.

Load your thinkorswim paperMoney chart with indicators set for your setup, watch the price action for 5 minutes during market hours, and make a decision: setup present or not — log your assessment in the journal either way.

Configure your thinkorswim paperMoney chart for your setup, then observe for 5 minutes — if you see a valid signal that meets all your rules, move to position 3; if not, log 'No clean setup' in your journal.
tasks.why: The discipline of sitting on your hands when there's no setup is worth more than any trade you'll make.

variants: If a valid setup appeared in position 2, execute your first real paper trade in thinkorswim paperMoney: write entry, stop, and target in journal BEFORE clicking buy, then place the trade and log the fill price and time.

Execute your first setup-based paper trade today: write the plan in your journal first (entry, stop, target), then place the order in thinkorswim paperMoney, set the stop immediately after fill, and log everything.

Place paper trade #1 — plan it in writing first (entry trigger, stop level, target), then open thinkorswim paperMoney and execute: limit order at entry, stop order set immediately, all details logged in journal including fill time.
tasks.why: Today is the first day your rules meet real-time execution — the journal entry makes it count.

variants: Close any open paper trade from yesterday in thinkorswim paperMoney and log the result in your Trading Journal: exit price, P/L in dollars, and whether it hit your target or stop.
Log yesterday's paper trade outcome in your journal: did price hit your stop, your target, or did you exit manually? Record the exact exit price and dollar P/L.
Complete yesterday's trade log in the Trading Journal — exit price, dollar P/L, whether you exited at stop/target/manual, and one word describing how you followed your rules.
tasks.why: Completing every trade log the next morning builds the accountability loop that improves performance.

variants: Open your setup's ticker on thinkorswim paperMoney, apply your indicators, and compare today's pre-market price action to yesterday's session — write one difference and one similarity in your journal.
Pull up your chosen ticker in thinkorswim paperMoney with your setup configured, look at yesterday vs. today's early action, and write two observations — one about price behavior, one about volume.
Load thinkorswim paperMoney with your ticker and setup, review yesterday's full session alongside early today, and write in your journal: what carried over and what changed overnight.
tasks.why: Comparing sessions back-to-back trains you to recognize pattern continuity vs. environment shifts.

variants: Look for a second paper trade today using your setup — if you find one that meets ALL rules, write the plan and place it; if not, write 'Sat out — no valid setup' in your journal and move on.

Scan your chart during market hours for a second paper trade opportunity: if your setup appears and every rule is met, place the trade and log it; if the setup is borderline, write 'Borderline — sat out' and skip it.

Make a go/no-go decision on paper trade #2 today: valid signal with volume confirmation and clean entry? Log and place it. Not clean? Write the reason you passed in your journal — sitting out is a win.
tasks.why: Passing on bad setups is a skill — every logged pass trains judgment.

variants: Count your paper trades so far in the Trading Journal and calculate your current win rate — wins divided by total trades, written as a percentage in your journal.
Open your Trading Journal and tally wins vs. losses across all paper trades so far — calculate win rate percentage and write it in a summary row at the top of the sheet.
Go to your Trading Journal, count total paper trades and winning trades, and write your win rate (wins/total × 100) in the journal — this is your first data point.
tasks.why: Tracking win rate from the start, even with 2-3 trades, builds the habit of objective self-assessment.

variants: Open SPY on thinkorswim paperMoney on a 5-minute chart and find 3 examples from the past 3 days where the setup appeared but wasn't clean — label each 'NO TRADE' in a screenshot and save them.
In thinkorswim paperMoney, load SPY on a 5-minute chart and identify 3 moments from the last 3 sessions where your setup looked close but failed at least one rule — screenshot and label each.
Pull up SPY 5-minute in thinkorswim paperMoney, scan the last 3 days, and find 3 near-signals that don't fully qualify — annotate them with the specific rule they failed and save the screenshots.

tasks.why: The penultimate session should look exactly like any other — consistency is the measure.

variants: Close any open positions from day 88 in thinkorswim paperMoney and complete the final regular journal entry — all fields, compliance score, debrief.
Log day 88's result: close open positions, write exit and P/L, compliance score, and debrief — this is the second-to-last log entry.
Open thinkorswim paperMoney, close positions from yesterday, fill in all journal fields, and write the debrief — you have one session left after today.
tasks.why: One session remaining — every log is complete before the final day.

variants: Open your Trading Journal and count your total paper trades — if below 40, today and tomorrow are must-trade sessions; if at 40+, write your final trade count and confirm the data set is complete.
Tally your final paper trade count and write it in a summary row — if you're at 40 or above, write 'Data set complete'; if below, plan to take every valid setup today and tomorrow.
Count every paper trade in your journal and write the total — 40 is the floor, 60 is the ceiling — write where you stand and what it means for your expectancy data set quality.
tasks.why: Going into day 90 knowing your trade count determines how to use the final session.

variants: Execute today's paper trade with full final-rulebook execution if a valid setup appears — this is the data point before the final review tomorrow.
Place today's paper trade if conditions clear under your final rulebook — memory execution, bracket exits in thinkorswim paperMoney, complete log, and debrief noting it's the final regular session.

Today's paper trade: apply final rulebook from memory, execute in thinkorswim paperMoney with bracket exits if conditions clear, log everything completely, and write in the debrief: 'Day 90 review is tomorrow.'
tasks.why: The last regular paper trade before the final review must be fully executed and logged.

variants: Open your Trading Journal and complete the Day 90 Final Milestone: total paper trades, all-time win rate, all-time expectancy, model trade average score, filter compliance average, and a one-paragraph assessment of whether your setup has positive expectancy.

Write your Day 90 Final Milestone in the Trading Journal with every performance metric — trade count, win rate, expectancy, average model trade score — and a one-paragraph verdict on whether you've built a viable setup.

Complete the Day 90 Milestone entry: write all performance numbers (trades, win rate, avg R:R, expectancy, model trade score), then write a definitive one-paragraph verdict — does your paper trading data support positive expectancy for this setup?
tasks.why: Day 90 is the final accounting — every number must be written and the expectancy verdict must be stated clearly.

variants: Write a 'What I Built' summary in your Trading Journal: list the 5 most valuable skills or habits you developed over 90 days — be specific, not generic ('I can identify my setup on a 5-minute chart in real time' not 'I learned trading').
Add a 'Skills Developed' section to your Trading Journal and write 5 specific, concrete skills you can now do that you couldn't on day 1 — named and defined, not vague.

Write a 'Day 1 vs. Day 90' comparison in your journal: 5 specific things you can do now — read charts, apply filters, execute with discipline, analyze performance data, sit out bad setups — that were impossible 90 days ago.
tasks.why: Naming your specific skills makes them real and portable — you'll remember what you built.

variants: Write your 'Next Steps' entry in the Trading Journal: based on your expectancy result, state clearly whether your setup is ready for more paper trading, needs refinement, or has earned the right to test with a very small real position someday — and what would have to be true for each outcome.

Document your path forward in the journal: if expectancy is positive, write the 3 conditions you'd need before ever considering real money; if not positive, write what you'd change in a second 90-day paper run.

Add a 'Path Forward' section to your Trading Journal: based on your day 90 data, write your next concrete step — whether that's 30 more paper trading sessions, a rulebook revision, or criteria for a future real account — and be specific about what readiness looks like.
tasks.why: Day 90 is not the end of trading — it's the end of phase 1; your next step needs to be documented before the momentum fades.
